candidate.title: Standardized proof envelope with MIME content-type for HTTP transport
candidate.description: Framework integrations currently serialize proofs as ad-hoc JSON blobs with no standard envelope. Define `application/bolyra-proof+json` with a fixed schema: `{version, circuit, publicSignals, proof, metadata: {prover, timestamp}}`. The TS and Python SDKs should produce and consume this format. This unblocks framework-agnostic middleware (FastAPI dependency, Express middleware, MCP tool wrapper) that can verify any Bolyra proof from the Content-Type header alone without knowing which framework generated it. Artifact: update `spec/draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01.md` §Wire-Format and add `sdk/src/envelope.ts` + `sdk-python/bolyra/envelope.py`.

candidate.title: Complete DID method resolution algorithm with deactivation semantics
candidate.description: The existing spec/did-method-bolyra.md defines the DID syntax but lacks a normative resolution algorithm. Define how did:bolyra:<commitment> resolves to a DID Document by querying IdentityRegistry on-chain state, including: (1) how revoked identities produce a deactivated DID Document per W3C DID Core §8.2, (2) how agent credentials populate verificationMethod with the operator's EdDSA key, (3) how delegation chains appear as delegateGrant entries. Deliverable: complete resolution algorithm in the DID method spec, plus a reference resolver in the TS SDK that returns conformant DID Documents.

candidate.title: Define off-chain session token format (JWT profile for verified handshakes)
candidate.description: After a successful on-chain handshake verification, there is no standard way to carry the verified session into HTTP/gRPC calls. Define a JWT profile where the claims include nullifierHash, scopeCommitment, sessionNonce, and a chain of delegationNullifiers. The token is signed by the relayer/verifier and carries a proof-receipt pointing to the on-chain transaction. This bridges Bolyra's on-chain verification with existing OAuth2/OIDC middleware. Deliverable: new section in the IETF draft specifying the JWT claim set, a reference implementation in the TS SDK, and 5 test vectors covering single-hop and delegated sessions.

candidate.title: Standard proof envelope with IANA-registerable content type
candidate.description: Define `application/bolyra-proof+cbor` as the canonical wire format for transmitting proofs between services. The envelope wraps proof bytes, public signals, circuit identifier, proving system (groth16/plonk), and an optional delegation chain array — everything a verifier needs in a single self-describing payload. Add `ProofEnvelope.encode()` and `ProofEnvelope.decode()` to both TS and Python SDKs. Without a standard envelope, every framework integration (LangChain, CrewAI, MCP) invents its own serialization, creating an interop nightmare as the ecosystem grows.

candidate.title: Define proof envelope MIME type and canonical serialization
candidate.description: Register application/bolyra-proof+cbor as a MIME type and define a canonical CBOR serialization for proof envelopes (human proof, agent proof, delegation chain). Currently the wire format is ad-hoc JSON with no schema versioning. A CBOR envelope with a version byte, deterministic field ordering, and content-type header enables interop between TS SDK, Python SDK, and third-party implementations. Deliverable: update draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01.md §Wire Format with CDDL schema, add encode/decode to both SDKs, and publish 10+ round-trip test vectors in spec/test-vectors/.

candidate.title: Add human tree root history buffer matching agent tree's 30-root buffer
candidate.description: IdentityRegistry maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree but humanTree has no such buffer — any new enrollment instantly invalidates in-flight human proofs. This is a correctness gap the IETF draft should normatively require: the contract comment even says 'humanTree uses Semaphore's' but the code doesn't actually use Semaphore's group contract with its root history. Add a 30-entry ring buffer for humanTree roots (mirroring the existing agentRootHistory pattern), update the verifyHandshake function to check against it, and add a normative requirement to the IETF draft §Security Considerations noting the staleness window.

candidate.title: Prove credential commitment uniquely binds all five fields
candidate.description: AgentPolicy computes credentialCommitment = Poseidon5(modelHash, opPkAx, opPkAy, permissionBitmask, expiryTimestamp). Poseidon with fixed arity is collision-resistant, but the circuit does not enforce that all five inputs are in the BN254 scalar field (only permissionBitmask and expiryTimestamp get Num2Bits range checks). modelHash and the pubkey components are unchecked — if modelHash exceeds the field modulus r, it wraps, creating two distinct preimages for the same commitment. Add Num2Bits(254) checks on modelHash (or assert it is a Poseidon output, which is inherently in-field), and write a property test demonstrating that no two distinct (modelHash, opPkAx, opPkAy, bitmask, expiry) tuples can produce the same credentialCommitment.

candidate.title: Machine-readable conformance test vector suite
candidate.description: The spec directory lacks machine-readable test vectors that a third-party implementer could run against their own SDK. Produce a spec/test-vectors/ directory with JSON files covering: valid handshake (happy path), expired agent credential, revoked human identity, stale Merkle root, scope subset violation, cumulative-bit encoding violation, delegation chain at depth 1/2/3, and nonce replay. Each vector includes input witnesses, expected public signals, and expected pass/fail. The existing circuit tests generate real witnesses — extract and freeze them as canonical vectors.
